column14: What do archaeological sites mean to people in coastal Bénin today?
column18: archaeology, climate change, tourism, place-based knowledge
column19: The coastal area of present-day Republic of Bénin contains archaeological and historical sites of global significance. Beyond the Bénin government's plans to make heritage-based tourism a lever of sustainable development, what do local tourism providers and local inhabitants think of this heritage?

column14: Central African Holocene Late Stone Age: Continuity and subsistence in an understudied region
column18: Central Africa, Late Stone Age, Subsistence
column19:
The Holocene Late Stone Age (LSA) of Central Africa remains one of the least systematically investigated archaeological records on the African continent, and analysis of more than 200 archaeological sites demonstrates that current interpretations are shaped by biased, fragmented datasets from geographically restricted excavations rather than by prehistoric reality itself.
At the same time, the evidence highlights the long-term coexistence of lithic technologies and ceramics, challenging traditional models that assume a linear transition from Stone Age to Iron Age and instead suggesting complex adaptive strategies within rainforest ecologies.
By integrating archaeological synthesis with emerging artefact analyses, including lipid and residue studies, I argue for regionally grounded interpretations of Central African Holocene societies based on their own archaeological evidence rather than externally imposed savannah-based models or ethnographic analogy.

column5: SAINSBURY RESEARCH UNIT, UNIVERSITY OF EAST ANGLIA UK
column14: Settlement and Landscape Architecture in Northern Yorùbáland, Nigeria: Recent Archaeological Research from the Pre-Medieval Period to the Recent Past
column18: Northern Yorubaland, Settlement development, Landscape architecture, pre-medieval archaeology, Nigeria
column19: This paper presents the results of recent archaeological investigations at two historically significant settlement sites in northern Yorùbáland, Nigeria; Ilorin and Shao; both associated with the history of the Oyo Empire, one of the most influential states in pre-colonial West Africa. Archaeological surveys, excavations, and radiocarbon dating reveal long-term occupation extending as far back as 10,000 BC at Ilorin and with distinctive potsherd pavement floors and from the seventeenth century onward at Shao, and a diverse material assemblage, including pottery, beads, cowries, glass, and imported wares, attesting to complex patterns of local development and long-distance interaction. The findings contribute to broader debates on settlement development, landscape architecture, sociopolitical transformation, and the historical trajectories of northern Yorùbáland within the wider context of West African archaeology.

column14: BEYOND PHOTOGRAPHS: EVALUATING STRUCTURE FROM MOTION (SFM) AND REFLECTANCE TRANSFORMATION IMAGING (RTI) FOR DIGITAL HERITAGE PRESERVATION IN THE AFRICAN ARCHAEOLOGY ARCHIVE COLOGNE (AAArC)
column18: 3D imaging, digital archives, cultural heritage preservation
column19: The African Archaeology Archive Cologne (AAArC) holds a substantial collection of documented African archaeological artefacts and heritage sites. While photography and technical drawings have been the primary medium for archaeological documentation in the past, they have limitations in capturing surface detail and three-dimensional morphology. This study examines the added value of 3D model creation through Structure from Motion (SfM), as well as Reflectance Transformation Imaging (RTI) within AAArC.

column5: Sainsbury Research Unit, University of East Anglia, UK
column14: Revisiting Liesegang’s Archaeological Archives, on the Old Kebbi Kingdom, Hausaland Nigeria
column19: This paper reassesses the archaeological archives and material assemblages generated by a major, but largely unpublished research project, funded by the German Research Foundation (DFG) and conducted in the Old Kebbi Kingdom in Sokoto State, Nigeria between 1990 and 1993. Titled “Towns, Villages, Hamlets and Ceramic Sequences in Old Kebbi ca. 500–1900 AD”, the project was conducted by Gerhard Liesegang, focused on the ‘history of historico-archaeological research on Kebbi’, was not focused on archaeology as an academic discipline, but rather as a larger historical project initiated by Prof Lange centred on urban structures seen as a way to study transformations in Hausaland and in some West African states. Due to lack of access to the finds as well as time limitations faced by Liesegang, the archaeological materials were not analysed or described to a higher level, which led the work to mainly rely on the field notes and plans. Having already located the archival records at the Frobenius Institute in Frankfurt, as well as the archaeological materials deposited at the National Commission for Museums and Monuments’ (NCMM) storage facility in Sokoto State, this PhD proposal seeks to reactivate the full archaeological potential of these data sets, through detailed and up-to-date analyses. This is with the aim of investigating the dynamics of the materials collected - trace evidence of trade, exchange, and material production within the Kebbi valley so as to reconstruct the region’s pre-colonial socio-economic networks.
